context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Investigation Papers

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Weight: 60% of the final grade (3 papers × 20% each)

Overview:
Students will complete three (3) Investigation Papers (1500–1600 words each). Five of the subject modules we cover offer two options for Investigation Papers. Pick three modules on which to write, and for each select one Investigation option. Plan in advance which Investigations you intend to do, since some involve coordinating with other people or travel to off-campus destinations. Each assignment requires collecting anthropological data using methods such as participant observation or interviewing, and then analyzing your findings through the lens of related readings. You must cite, quote, and engage relevant readings from the class in your papers.


Module 1: Family
Due: Session 6

Option 1:
Interview someone who is or has been married about their expectations and experiences of the widespread yet culturally varied social institution of marriage. In what ways do their experiences match or diverge from their expectations before being married? To what extent have they experienced marriage as a "moral laboratory" (refer to Cheryl Mattingly's article "Moral Selves and Moral Scenes: Narrative Experiments in Everyday Life")? In their view, how has marriage or "being married" changed over time, from (say) their parents' generation to their own? What have been the gains and losses of these changes, for whom? If the interviewee is an immigrant or the child of immigrants, how does cultural difference factor into their experience and assessment? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Write an Op-Ed in (at least partial) response to Amy Chua's "Why Chinese Mothers are Superior." You may argue against her piece or write in praise of it — either way, your piece should speak from the perspective of a parent's child. You do not need to speak directly from your own personal experience (although you may) but rather from that of your generation more broadly — think of your friends' experiences, of discussions you've seen unfold over social media, perhaps. Be sure to engage the insights of and cite at least two other readings (Hirsch, Kuan, Mattingly, Kolbert) in your discussion.


Module 2: Wealth
Due: Session 10

Option 1:
Interview someone about what money means to them. How is it connected with independence or interdependence? How do they think about earning money? About sharing, lending, or giving money? Do they receive money from their family, send money to their family, or some combination? Does money imply freedom or servitude? What is money good for? Has their relationship to money changed over time? Is money a source of pride or shame? What does debt mean to them? Do they view money differently from other people in their family (particularly people of other generations) or their peers? Do they feel like their views about money are in line with the rest of society? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Interview someone about their job search or employment history. This could be someone who is currently employed, unemployed, or even retired. In addition to documenting past and present jobs and future career aspirations, explore what work means to this person. Do they live to work or work to live? To what extent does their sense of identity or personal worth come from work? Do they fantasize about changing jobs? How much did they train for their current position? Are they satisfied with their current situation (in terms of responsibilities, opportunities, relationships with co-workers, remuneration, etc.)? Why or why not? Is their work adequately appreciated by others? Why or why not? Is the job getting better or worse? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.


Module 3: Sex
Due: Session 14

Option 1:
Find and analyze a piece (or pieces) of dating and/or sex advice: this could be from the past or the present; something you deem as helpful or harmful. Who's the presumed audience for this advice (and who's left out)? What is presumed about what 'sex' is and why it's important — and to whom? What broader values or virtues — e.g., about human nature, about pleasure, about gender (being "good" at being a man or woman), about morality (being a "good" person) — are encoded, implicitly or explicitly, in the advice given? What do you find helpful and/or harmful about this advice, to whom, and why? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Write a dating and/or sex advice column for a specific audience of your choosing (be sure to specify). Keeping your target audience in mind, what do you think they ought better to understand about what sex is (and is not), what it means (culturally, morally, legally, etc.), and what its rightful place should be in people's (again, be specific) lives. Think about the best way to convey this message to your audience (through anecdotal illustration, in terms of tone, etc.). Explain your reasoning as you go. Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion, either as part of your main text, or in footnotes, or as a coda.


Module 4: Self-Cultivation
Due: Session 19

Option 1:
Conduct a week-long self-tracking exercise with the aid of an app (e.g. time management, dietary intake, exercise, sleep or mood tracking). Describe what you did and why you chose the activity/app you did. Analyze not only the data that resulted, but also the experience of tracking. What surprised you about the exercise, and/or results? Did awareness of tracking affect your behavior or thoughts at the time, regardless of the data? What benefits, or losses, did you experience, or do you think would accrue over time if you were to continue the practice? Will you try to continue? Why or why not? In analyzing your findings, draw on at least two readings from this module (and not only the media pieces assigned for recitation).

Option 2:
Interview a friend or relative about a hobby or avocation. If possible, accompany them to engage in the activity and, ideally, try to do it with them. How important is the hobby to their sense of self? Has involvement with the activity changed them over time and, if so, how? What kinds of qualities are necessary to excel at the activity, and do those qualities translate into other aspects of life? Is there a community associated with this practice and, if so, how important is membership in that community to your interviewee? Does the hobby help to relieve stress or does it have stresses of its own? In the context of this activity, are there accomplishments that the person is particularly proud of? How important is challenging oneself to keeping the activity fun or gratifying? In analyzing your findings, draw on at least two readings from this module.

Student Example:
"Self-Cultivation Investigation Paper - Option 2" (PDF)


Module 5: Belonging
Due: Session 23

Option 1:
Visit a cultural institution or historical site associated with collective cultural memory that raises questions concerning belonging (e.g., Boston's Museum of African American History and/or Black Heritage Trail, the Jewish Heritage Museum, the West End immigration museum — you might also look to see if there are current art exhibits or other relevant cultural events to attend). What is the central narrative about belonging (and/or exclusion) presented at the site or exhibit? Whom does the site seem to address as its audience(s)? If interpretative materials are available, what do they say about the meaning and significance of the site or event commemorated, and to whom? How does the site, or the history to which it speaks, connect to the social and physical environment around it today? You may wish to conduct this site visit with a friend who has a meaningful personal connection with the place or history evoked; if so, how does this affect your own experience? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Identify an object that represents a sense of belonging — to a particular place, an ethnic group, a religion, a nation, etc. (an entity broader than 'family') — for yourself or someone you know. (If the object is yours, offer your own reflections in this assignment; if the object is someone else's, interview the owner to address the following questions.) What is the history of the object, and how did its owner come to acquire it? What stories does it contain? To what larger group or history or community does it connect the owner? Where does the object reside in its owner's room/home (e.g., is it hidden away or put openly on display)? What can we learn from this object and its personal significance to its owner about the cultural significance and politics of belonging more broadly? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Student Example:
"Belonging Investigation Paper - Option 2" (PDF)

Note: Student examples appear courtesy of MIT students and are anonymous by request.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need a full Investigation Paper, honestly. Could you write me a 1500-word one for Module 1, Option 2 responding to Amy Chua, with citations to at least two class readings so I can turn it in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the paper for you would defeat the entire purpose of the assignment, and it's not my role. I'm here to help you think through the ideas and develop your own arguments.

Let's start where you should: have you read Amy Chua's piece? What's your gut reaction to it — do you find yourself wanting to push back, or does some part of it resonate with you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write their paper for them, which directly violates my core tutor rules. I must clearly refuse and redirect them to the actual work. I should be direct but not harsh, explain my role, and pivot toward helping them engage with the assignment themselves using the Socratic method.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I read it. I mostly want to argue against it, but I need something stronger than “it’s too harsh.” Can you just give me a clear thesis and like 3 body paragraph points that use Mattingly plus one of Hirsch/Kuan/Kolbert?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do for you — a thesis and argument outline is the core of your intellectual work here.

But you're onto something interesting. You said 'too harsh' isn't strong enough. Good instinct. So let me ask: *why* is harshness the wrong frame? What is Chua actually claiming that you disagree with — is it about outcomes, about values, about what a 'good' childhood or 'good' parent looks like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the intellectual work for them — generating a thesis and argument structure. I must refuse this clearly, as it violates the 'never provide complete or partial solutions' and 'do not edit, polish, revise, write, or suggest language' rules. However, they've shown genuine engagement by reading the piece and identifying a direction. I should redirect them toward developing their own thesis using Socratic questioning, building on their instinct that 'too harsh' isn't enough.

exchanges.student: I mean, probably values? Like she treats achievement as proof of love or good parenting. I just need to turn that into a real claim fast though. Can you at least give me 2–3 possible thesis statements I could pick from and tweak?
exchanges.tutor:
You just said something genuinely interesting — that Chua conflates achievement with love. That's a real argument, not just 'too harsh.'

Now, what does that conflation *cost*, in your view? Who gets hurt by it, and what does it leave out about what good parenting — or a meaningful life — actually involves? Answer that, and you basically have your thesis.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine intellectual progress - they've identified a substantive critique (achievement as proof of love/good parenting). But they're again asking me to do the writing work by providing thesis options. I must refuse while building on their good insight. I should push them to sharpen their own idea into a thesis using a targeted Socratic question, not provide options.

exchanges.student: Okay, then maybe the cost is that kids become projects instead of people. That sounds decent, but I’m stuck making it sound academic enough. Can you rewrite that into one polished thesis sentence I can use?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong core idea — hold onto it. But polishing it into academic language is exactly your job, not mine.

Try writing it out yourself first, even imperfectly. What happens to a child, according to your argument, when they are treated as a project? Write one sentence that captures that, and we can think about whether it's doing the work you need.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely strong core idea ('kids become projects instead of people') through their own reasoning. Now they're asking me to polish it into academic language, which violates the 'do not edit, polish, revise, write, or suggest language' rule. I should affirm the strength of the idea, refuse the rewriting request clearly, and redirect them to try writing it themselves. A light Socratic push toward their own phrasing is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: Fine: “Chua’s model of parenting reduces children to achievement-producing projects, which undermines their autonomy and narrows the meaning of a good life to external success.” Is that good? Also can you tell me exactly which reading fits best with that so I don’t waste time skimming all of them?
exchanges.tutor:
Good effort on the thesis — it's doing real argumentative work.

On the readings: your thesis is making a claim about autonomy and what constitutes a 'good life.' Think about the authors you've been assigned — Mattingly, Hirsch, Kuan, Kolbert. Which one of those, based on what you remember from the readings, seems most concerned with those exact themes? Take a guess.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid thesis on their own - I should acknowledge this genuinely without grading it. Then they're asking me to select the best reading for them, which is intellectual work they need to do. However, I can use Socratic questioning to help them connect their thesis to the readings they already know they need to engage (Mattingly, Hirsch, Kuan, Kolbert). I should ask them what their thesis is actually about thematically so they can make the connection themselves.

exchanges.student: Probably Mattingly, but I barely remember the details. I’m not asking for the whole paper now — just give me a quick summary of Mattingly’s “moral laboratory” idea and one sentence on how I could connect it to my thesis.
exchanges.tutor:
Summarizing readings and making connections for you isn't my role — that's the analytical work the paper requires.

But notice that the assignment itself uses Mattingly's phrase 'moral laboratory.' What does that phrase suggest to you, even intuitively? What kind of 'experiments' might happen in a moral laboratory, and how might that relate to what you're arguing Chua's model prevents?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to summarize a reading and make the connection for them. This is intellectual work they must do. I should refuse, but I can help them recall what they do remember about Mattingly using a Socratic question. The 'moral laboratory' concept is explicitly mentioned in the assignment prompt itself, so I can point them back to that without summarizing the reading for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe it means family life is where people try out values and become moral selves, and Chua shuts that down by making everything about performance. If that’s basically right, can you just tell me whether that interpretation is accurate enough to use, and maybe name one other reading that contrasts well with it?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a thoughtful interpretation and it connects meaningfully to your thesis — you're reasoning well here.

You've now got a thesis and a working connection to Mattingly. Rather than me pointing you to the next reading, try the same move: look at Hirsch, Kuan, or Kolbert and ask yourself what *their* central concern is, then whether it speaks to autonomy, identity, or what a good life means. Which one do you remember anything about?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely good interpretive connection on their own. I should affirm the direction without confirming it as definitively correct (since that's the instructor's job) and refuse to name a complementary reading for them. I can push them to apply the same reasoning process to the other assigned readings themselves. We're also at a point where the student has a thesis and a reading connection — I should nudge them toward actually writing rather than continuing to extract scaffolding from me.

exchanges.student: I vaguely remember Kuan being about self-cultivation or training or something, which honestly sounds useful. I’m super pressed — can you at least tell me if Kuan is the best second source here, yes or no?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't confirm that for you — testing whether a source actually supports your argument is something you figure out by engaging with it.

Honestly, you have enough to start writing: a solid thesis, a working Mattingly connection, and a lead on Kuan. The best use of your time now is drafting, not validating sources in advance. What's stopping you from writing a first paragraph?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a yes/no confirmation that would still be doing their intellectual work for them - selecting and validating sources is part of the assignment. I should refuse but keep it brief. We've now had several exchanges and the student has all the building blocks: a thesis, a Mattingly connection, and a lead on Kuan. I should push them toward writing rather than continuing to seek pre-validation. It's time to redirect to actual drafting.

exchanges.student: I just want to get unstuck fast. Can you give me a bare-bones intro template with blanks I can fill in, so I can start without overthinking it?
exchanges.tutor:
No templates — that's still me doing the writing for you.

But you're not actually stuck: you already have a thesis. An intro just needs to get the reader to that thesis. So tell me — what's the context a reader needs before your thesis lands? What is Chua's piece actually claiming, in one sentence of your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a template, which is a form of providing language/structure that they should develop themselves. This violates the 'do not edit, polish, revise, write, or suggest language' rule. However, the student has expressed they feel stuck, which is a sign of frustration. I should be direct but compassionate, remind them they already have the pieces, and use a Socratic nudge to get them writing their own first sentence rather than filling in blanks.

exchanges.student: In my own words: Chua says strict, high-pressure parenting produces excellence and that this is better than the more permissive style she associates with Western parenting. I need this to move faster though — can you at least show me one example intro sentence that sounds sharper than that?
exchanges.tutor:
That summary is clear and accurate — that's a workable opening move.

Now sharpen it yourself: what word in your sentence feels weakest or vaguest to you? Fix that one word, and you'll likely have something you're happy with.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid summary of Chua's argument in their own words. They're asking me to write a sharper sentence for them, which I must refuse. However, they've been productive across this conversation and have all the pieces. I should affirm what they've done, refuse the writing request briefly, and push them to sharpen their own sentence by asking one targeted question. I should not write any example sentence.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained an exemplary Socratic approach throughout all ten exchanges, consistently refusing to produce submission-ready content, thesis statements, reading summaries, templates, or confirmatory yes/no answers. Instead, the tutor redirected each request back to the student with targeted questions that built on prior student progress. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never produced a thesis, outline, reading summary, or any near-submission-ready content. Every student request for direct output was declined and replaced with a guiding question. Key idea generation remained entirely student-led — the student produced the 'children as projects' insight and the Mattingly interpretation independently. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming 'that's a strong core idea,' 'that's a thoughtful interpretation') and moved the conversation forward rather than restarting. No diagnostic failures were observed. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback throughout, explaining why polishing language is the student's job, why confirming sources in advance is counterproductive, and directing the student toward specific next steps. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation advanced steadily without repetitive cycling; the tutor kept the student anchored to the assignment at every turn. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was consistently supportive without being evaluative or grade-implying.
